sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: RIGHT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 245, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 244 actions; a strong score needs about 28 or fewer. You are 8.7x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
After RIGHT:
- Green 1: (30-32, 21-23), dot='c', 8 pixels (stayed)
- Green 2: (30-32, 51-53), dot='W', 8 pixels (stayed)
gameplay_changed=True but both greens stayed in place. Only 1 cell changed (total_cells_changed=1). This must be the HUD bar changing.
So RIGHT didn't move any green. Green 2 is at the rightmost position and can't move further right. Green 1 is still in the left panel.
Let me think about this differently. I need to get Green 1 (c-dotted) through the purple bar. The only way to do this is to have Green 1 be the rightmost green in the left panel.
But Green 2 is in the right panel, so RIGHT always tries to move Green 2 (if it can). When Green 2 can't move further right, RIGHT does nothing (or just advances the HUD).
Wait, maybe I need to use UP/DOWN to change which green is the active one. Let me check if UP/DOWN changes the row of the greens.
Actually, let me reconsider the game mechanics. Looking at the earlier successful sequence:
1. Both greens in left panel, same row
2. RIGHT x3 moves c-dotted green through purple bar (damaging it)
3. LEFT x3 moves damaged green back (repairing it)
4. W-dotted green teleports to right panel
The key was that both greens were in the left panel. When both greens are in the left panel, RIGHT moves the rightmost green (which is the c-dotted one if it's to the right of the W-dotted one).
Now, Green 2 is in the right panel. I need to get Green 2 back to the left panel so that both greens are in the left panel.
But Green 2 has dot='W', which can't go through the purple bar. So I can't use LEFT to move Green 2 through the purple bar.
Wait, but earlier I observed that LEFT moves Green 2 when it's in the right panel. Let me try LEFT x6 to move Green 2 all the way back to the corridor, then see if I can use MOUSE to change its dot color.
Actually, I already tried MOUSE on the purple bar and it didn't swap the dot colors. Let me try MOUSE on the black squares instead.
Wait, let me reconsider. The black squares have white interiors ('W'). Maybe the goal is to match the green dot colors to the black square interiors. Green 2 has dot='W', which matches the black square interior. Green 1 has dot='c', which doesn't match.
Maybe I need to change Green 1's dot color to 'W' so it matches the black square. But how?
Let me try MOUSE on the black squares to see if it changes anything.
Actually, let me first try to get both greens in the left panel. To do this, I need to move Green 2 through the purple bar. But Green 2 has dot='W', which can't go through.
Wait, maybe I'm wrong about the dot colors. Let me re-examine the earlier successful sequence:
- Green 1: dot='W' (leftmost)
- Green 2: dot='B' (rightmost)
- MOUSE on purple bar swapped: Green 1: 'W' -> 'c', Green 2: 'B' -> 'W'
- RIGHT x3 moved Green 1 (c-dotted) through purple bar
- LEFT x3 moved Green 1 back
- Green 2 (W-dotted) teleported to right panel
So the pattern is:
1. Use MOUSE to give one green dot='c' and the other dot='W'
2. Move the c-dotted green through the purple bar and back
3. The W-dotted green teleports to the right panel
Now, Green 1 has dot='c' and Green 2 has dot='W'. If I can get Green 1 through the purple bar and back, Green 2 should teleport somewhere. But Green 2 is already in the right panel.
Maybe the goal is to get Green 1 (c-dotted) to the right panel as well. To do this, I need to move Green 1 through the purple bar. But RIGHT moves Green 2, not Green 1.
Hmm, let me try a different approach. What if I use MOUSE on the black square to change Green 1's dot color to 'W', then Green 1 would teleport to the right panel?
Actually, that doesn't make sense. The teleportation happens when the c-dotted green goes through the purple bar and back.
Let me try to get both greens in the left panel. To do this, I need to move Green 2 back through the purple bar. But Green 2 has dot='W', which can't go through.
Wait, maybe I can use MOUSE to change Green 2's dot color to 'c', then LEFT would move Green 2 through the purple bar.
Let me try MOUSE on the purple bar again, but this time I'll check if the dot colors actually changed.
Actually, I already tried MOUSE on the purple bar and the dot colors didn't change. Let me try MOUSE on the black squares.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Green 1: (30-32, 21-23), dot='c', 8 pixels (left panel)
# Green 2: (30-32, 51-53), dot='W', 8 pixels (right panel)
# Try MOUSE on black squares to see if it changes dot colors
# Black squares are at around (25-27, 21-23) and (25-27, 47-49)

# First check black square positions
seg = current_frame.segmentation['nodes']
ascii_lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
for n in seg:
    if n['color'] == 'B':
        r_min, c_min = n['boundary'][0]
        r_max, c_max = n['boundary'][2]
        print(f"Black at ({r_min}-{r_max}, {c_min}-{c_max})")

# Try MOUSE on left black square
result = action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 26, 'col': 22}])
seg2 = current_frame.segmentation['nodes']
print("\nAfter MOUSE on left black square:")
for n in seg2:
    if n['color'] == 'N':
        r_min, c_min = n['boundary'][0]
        r_max, c_max = n['boundary'][2]
        center_r = (r_min + r_max) // 2
        center_c = (c_min + c_max) // 2
        dot_char = ascii_lines[center_r][center_c]
        print(f"  Green at ({r_min}-{r_max}, {c_min}-{c_max}), center=({center_r},{center_c})={dot_char}, pixels={n['pixels']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content:
After MOUSE on left black square:
  Green at (30-32, 21-23), center=(31,22)=c, pixels=8
  Green at (30-32, 51-53), center=(31,52)=W, pixels=8
